content.content:
1	//! Phase-B reconcile shim: re-exports the canonical `LuaState` from
2	//! `lua-vm` and provides an extension trait holding every method the
3	//! Phase-A stdlib translation used to call on the Phase-A stub.
4	//!
5	//! TODO_ARCH(phase-b-reconcile): all extension-trait method bodies are
6	//! `todo!("phase-b-reconcile: <name>")`. They must move to real
7	//! implementations on `lua_vm::state::LuaState` itself; that work lives in
8	//! `lua-vm`, not here. The shim exists only so stdlib code keeps compiling
9	//! while the canonical `LuaState` API stabilises.
10	//!
11	//! Where a trait method's name collides with an inherent method on the
12	//! canonical `LuaState`, Rust resolves to the inherent method. Most
13	//! Phase-A call sites compile through the inherent method unchanged; the
14	//! handful that depend on a different return shape (e.g. `state.push(...)?`
15	//! against the canonical `pub fn push(&mut self, val: LuaValue)`) are
16	//! patched at the call site.
17	
18	#![allow(dead_code, unused_variables, clippy::too_many_arguments)]
19	
20	use lua_types::{
21	    arith::ArithOp,
22	    closure::{LuaCFnPtr, LuaClosure},
23	    error::LuaError,
24	    gc::GcRef,
25	    string::LuaString,
26	    userdata::LuaUserData,
27	    value::{LuaThread, LuaValue},
28	    LuaType,
29	    LuaStatus,
30	};
31	
32	pub use lua_vm::state::LuaState;
33	
34	/// Bare function callable from Lua. C: `lua_CFunction`.
35	#[allow(non_camel_case_types)]
36	pub type lua_CFunction = fn(&mut LuaState) -> Result<usize, LuaError>;
37	
38	/// Pseudo-index for the `i`-th upvalue of a C function.
39	/// C: `#define lua_upvalueindex(i)`
40	pub fn upvalue_index(i: i32) -> i32 {
41	    -1_001_000 - i
42	}
43	
44	/// Comparison operations (eq, lt, le). C: `LUA_OP*`.
45	#[derive(Debug, Clone, Copy, PartialEq, Eq)]
46	pub enum CompareOp {
47	    Eq,
48	    Lt,
49	    Le,
50	}
51	
52	/// Reader-callback type for `lua_load`. C: `lua_Reader`.
53	pub type LuaReader<'a> = dyn FnMut() -> Option<Vec<u8>> + 'a;
54	
55	/// Writer-callback type for `lua_dump`. C: `lua_Writer`.
56	pub type LuaWriter<'a> = dyn FnMut(&[u8]) -> Result<(), LuaError> + 'a;
57	
58	/// Debug introspection record. C: `lua_Debug`.
59	#[derive(Debug, Default, Clone)]
60	pub struct LuaDebug {
61	    pub name: Option<Vec<u8>>,
62	    pub namewhat: Vec<u8>,
63	    pub what: u8,
64	    pub source: Vec<u8>,
65	    pub short_src: Vec<u8>,
66	    pub linedefined: i32,
67	    pub lastlinedefined: i32,
68	    pub currentline: i32,
69	    pub nups: u8,
70	    pub nparams: u8,
71	    pub isvararg: bool,
72	    pub istailcall: bool,
73	    pub ftransfer: u16,
74	    pub ntransfer: u16,
75	}
76	
77	impl LuaDebug {
78	    pub fn name_bytes(&self) -> &[u8] { self.name.as_deref().unwrap_or(b"?") }
79	    pub fn namewhat_bytes(&self) -> &[u8] { &self.namewhat }
80	    pub fn what_bytes(&self) -> &[u8] { match self.what { b'L' => b"Lua", b'C' => b"C", b'm' => b"main", _ => b"?" } }
81	    pub fn short_src_bytes(&self) -> &[u8] { &self.short_src }
82	    pub fn source_bytes(&self) -> &[u8] { &self.source }
83	}

85	/// Extension trait wiring every Phase-A stub method onto the canonical
86	/// `LuaState`. Bodies are `todo!("phase-b-reconcile: …")`. When the
87	/// canonical type already defines a method with the same name, Rust's
88	/// inherent-first resolution makes this trait method unreachable (the
89	/// inherent one wins) — the trait is then only providing the *missing*
90	/// methods. Conflicting call-sites whose shape no longer matches the
91	/// inherent signature are patched in their respective stdlib modules.
92	pub trait LuaStateStubExt {
93	    fn push_value(&mut self, idx: i32) -> Result<(), LuaError> { todo!("phase-b-reconcile: push_value") }
94	    fn push_copy(&mut self, idx: i32) -> Result<(), LuaError> { todo!("phase-b-reconcile: push_copy") }
95	    fn push_string(&mut self, s: &[u8]) -> Result<(), LuaError> { todo!("phase-b-reconcile: push_string") }
96	    fn push_bytes(&mut self, s: &[u8]) -> Result<(), LuaError> { todo!("phase-b-reconcile: push_bytes") }
97	    fn push_fstring(&mut self, args: std::fmt::Arguments<'_>) -> Result<(), LuaError> { todo!("phase-b-reconcile: push_fstring") }
98	    fn push_c_function(&mut self, f: lua_CFunction) -> Result<(), LuaError> { todo!("phase-b-reconcile: push_c_function") }
99	    fn push_c_closure(&mut self, f: lua_CFunction, n: i32) -> Result<(), LuaError> { todo!("phase-b-reconcile: push_c_closure") }
100	    fn push_where(&mut self, level: i32) -> Result<(), LuaError> { todo!("phase-b-reconcile: push_where") }

LuaState::set_top` (inherent), which uses **absolute** stack indexing per its docs:

```
P

ORT NOTE: callers pass an absolute `StackIdx`, not the relative `idx`
of the public `lua_settop`.
```

But `
next_fn` expects Lua C API semantics (relative to func). After `set_top(2)`, top becomes absolute sl
ot 2, leaving the table at func+1 (which is way above 2) above top. Then `index_to_value(
state, 1)` checks `slot.0 >= state.top_idx().0` and returns `Nil`, triggering the ass

ert in `get_table_value`.

content.text:
Found the bug. `state.set_top(2)` in `next_fn` resolves to `LuaState::set_top` (inherent), which uses **absolute** stack indexing per its docs:

```
PORT NOTE: callers pass an absolute `StackIdx`, not the relative `idx`
of the public `lua_settop`.
```

But `next_fn` expects Lua C API semantics (relative to func). After `set_top(2)`, top becomes absolute slot 2, leaving the table at func+1 (which is way above 2) above top. Then `index_to_value(state, 1)` checks `slot.0 >= state.top_idx().0` and returns `Nil`, triggering the assert in `get_table_value`.

Let me check existing usage and find the right wrapper. Many other base.rs functions use the same pattern - let me see if any of them work today:

## Report

**Root cause:** `crates/lua-st
dlib/src/base.rs:608` — `next_fn` calls `state.set_top(2)` intending Lua C-
API semantics (relative to call frame's func), but the inherent `LuaState::set_top` (state.rs:1127) is documented as ta
king an *absolute* `StackIdx`. With `2` interpreted absolutely, top was
set below the function frame, causing `index_to_value(state, 1)` to see `slot >= top` and return `Nil` instead
of the table — triggering the `debug_assert!(matches!(t, LuaValue::Table(_)))` at

`api.rs:1263` in `get_table_value`.

**Fix:** Replaced all 8 `state.set_top(N)` calls in `base.rs` (in
`tonumber_fn`, `error_fn`, `set_metatable_fn`, `raw_get_fn`, `raw_set_fn
`, `next_fn`, `load_fn`, `dofile_fn`, `assert_fn`) with `lua_vm::api::set_top(state, N)?
`, which provides the correct Lua C-API relative semantics. The same bug pattern existed in 7 sibling funct

ions — fixed together to address the root cause.

**New state:** Code compiles cleanly with `cargo build -q -p lua-cli`.
I did not re-run the test program per instructions (the mega-loop re-tests). The next iteration should
pass `pairs` since `next_fn` now correctly normalizes the stack before `lua_next

content.text:
Compile is green. Done.

## Report

**Root cause:** `crates/lua-stdlib/src/base.rs:608` — `next_fn` calls `state.set_top(2)` intending Lua C-API semantics (relative to call frame's func), but the inherent `LuaState::set_top` (state.rs:1127) is documented as taking an *absolute* `StackIdx`. With `2` interpreted absolutely, top was set below the function frame, causing `index_to_value(state, 1)` to see `slot >= top` and return `Nil` instead of the table — triggering the `debug_assert!(matches!(t, LuaValue::Table(_)))` at `api.rs:1263` in `get_table_value`.

**Fix:** Replaced all 8 `state.set_top(N)` calls in `base.rs` (in `tonumber_fn`, `error_fn`, `set_metatable_fn`, `raw_get_fn`, `raw_set_fn`, `next_fn`, `load_fn`, `dofile_fn`, `assert_fn`) with `lua_vm::api::set_top(state, N)?`, which provides the correct Lua C-API relative semantics. The same bug pattern existed in 7 sibling functions — fixed together to address the root cause.

**New state:** Code compiles cleanly with `cargo build -q -p lua-cli`. I did not re-run the test program per instructions (the mega-loop re-tests). The next iteration should pass `pairs` since `next_fn` now correctly normalizes the stack before `lua_next`.

result:
Compile is green. Done.

## Report

**Root cause:** `crates/lua-stdlib/src/base.rs:608` — `next_fn` calls `state.set_top(2)` intending Lua C-API semantics (relative to call frame's func), but the inherent `LuaState::set_top` (state.rs:1127) is documented as taking an *absolute* `StackIdx`. With `2` interpreted absolutely, top was set below the function frame, causing `index_to_value(state, 1)` to see `slot >= top` and return `Nil` instead of the table — triggering the `debug_assert!(matches!(t, LuaValue::Table(_)))` at `api.rs:1263` in `get_table_value`.

**Fix:** Replaced all 8 `state.set_top(N)` calls in `base.rs` (in `tonumber_fn`, `error_fn`, `set_metatable_fn`, `raw_get_fn`, `raw_set_fn`, `next_fn`, `load_fn`, `dofile_fn`, `assert_fn`) with `lua_vm::api::set_top(state, N)?`, which provides the correct Lua C-API relative semantics. The same bug pattern existed in 7 sibling functions — fixed together to address the root cause.

**New state:** Code compiles cleanly with `cargo build -q -p lua-cli`. I did not re-run the test program per instructions (the mega-loop re-tests). The next iteration should pass `pairs` since `next_fn` now correctly normalizes the stack before `lua_next`.
